item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as it comes from here with no indication it has been released under Creative Commons. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Downvoted. Needlessly wiping out an harmless anomaly is more the GOC's style. It's contain and protect, not destroy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yet another example for newbies on the site showing why the Foundation doesn't just kill things instead of containing them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think there needs to be a warning or something for the sad ones. My anxiety is set off quite easily by things like this, especially since I have this strange emotional attachment to all birds.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So this is a very good article. However, if the bird is not particularly intelligent, how is it any more anomalous than a parrot?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They act like they don't know why the animal is distressed

This is, unfortunately, part of clinical tone. Unless the bird outright said "hey, you killed all my friends" or something similar to that, they can't "officially" say why the bird is distressed. Because the assumption might be wrong, even though the chances of it in this case are virtually nil.

There's a story on the Darwin Awards like this (their site is currently down, otherwise I'd link it). The medical technicians in the report to a medical journal they submitted literally could not say that the beer-filled condoms some guy swallowed on a drunken dare was actually filled with beer, because they didn't test to confirm that it was, in fact, beer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I acknowledge that I'm responding to a deadpost, but ^this is an important point. To be fair, though, the Foundation's holy crap kill it kill it now instinct is justifiably very strong. I'm just upset that this article made no effort to explain why they killed the colony—even a token "this is creepy" would have improved it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …And they exterminated the -A colony why, exactly?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Bloody Bureaucracy…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I hate articles like this, not because they show the Foundation being unnecessarily cruel, but because they show the Foundation being unnecessarily stupid. They act like they don't know why the animal is distressed, and take overly-extreme measures to contain something that, at best, belongs on the Log of Anomalous Items.

This article feels like it's contriving tragedies to make us feel sorry for this thing by making it seem like the entire Foundation has been living off of lead paint for the past decade. The Foundation is cold, not cruel, and this feels cruel and stupid.

Downvoted.

item.title: Re: ... really foundation?
item.content:encoded:
Why not go exterminate the world's parrots ya douches?

If the world's parrots were all anomalous, the Foundation might. Unfortunately, that's how science works sometimes—to find out how or why something works, people break them beyond fixing. It's not all walking away from explosions and blowing up unstable chemicals in labs here. There are other examples of the Foundation messing up living creatures, simply because they didn't know better. They're human, after all.

item.title: ... really foundation?
item.content:encoded:
You found a seagull and all it's friends, and then you kill the entire colony except the leader, put it under mental trauma, drag it to a secret base in who knows where keeping it locked with a bunch of other freaks and mutants never to see the light of day again. Then you go around doing tests to find out why it barely moves or eats.

All because they were birds that could make human noises.

Why not go exterminate the world's parrots ya douches?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Poor thing :(

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: well that was unexpected. I frowned for a good minute before I even realized I was physically reacting to it. I guess you earned an up-vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man, sometimes I wonder why it takes me so long to realize that drafts I liked actually got posted. Nice work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I initially found it very out of character of the Foundation to try and make a skip happy, but the final punch to the gut in the Acquisition Log makes me wonder: Is the Foundation trying to make up for making this bird's existence a living hell?

Either way, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
capable of vocalizing non-lingual human vocal cues, such as growling, sighing, and weeping.

So it can create human sounding, non human sounds? What I mean is, how did we identify that the growl sounded human, compared to say, a dog growling?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 on this post!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article turned out to be a lot sadder than I thought it would be. +1!

As a side note, the idea of the Foundation having an "Avian Psychological Board" is both fascinating and depressing. Apparently psychologically unhealthy birds are a common enough problem within the Foundation that it warrants its own board.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now I want the Foundation to try giving it MDMA.

For the article: you nearly made me cry, damn you. +1 :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oops! Thanks for catching that. I'm actually taking a psych class right now and I totally knew that. xD Leave it to me to bone up the science.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I upvoted, but I have to note one issue about the science:

Biweekly, SCP-2550 is to be administered specially designed Selective Serotonin Reuptake Inhibitors (SSRIs) designed to alter the brain chemistry of the Phoebastria albatrus to increase serotonin production.

SSRI medications specifically block the destruction of serotonin, not increase the production-it's in the name. L-tryptophan increases production, and drugs that increase the release of serotonin include dextromethorphan, methadone, and ecstasy. I recommend changing the last word to "levels" instead of "production". I know it is picayune, but it is better science.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is depressing, but good. It almost seems more like an anomalous object than a scip though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2550 is most often observed sighing, suggesting moderate emotional distress, if its vocalizations thematically resemble human meanings.

I want to point this sentence out. For starters, it's already stated further up in the description that the bird doesn't seem to be more intelligent than normal for its species, so this supposition would be counterindicated.

lol big words

Speaking narratively, whether or not the thing is actually vocalizing its own emotions is immaterial; it'd be a whole lot better if you just let those vocalization patterns be mentioned through the article, creating the story, than telling us what they mean. In other words, let the reader draw their own conclusion as to what, if anything, the vocalizations mean.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not the best thing I've ever read, but interesting enough to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Holy crap just throw a few more birds in there guys.

Like, it's not that hard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If I could change one thing about this article;

SCP-2550 is to be kept in a standard grade avian containment installation at Site-24's "The Aviary",

I think that's grammatically correct, but it breaks the flow of the article and just feels off. Since it appears that the site indeed does have an aviary it might just be better to call it one and not give it a title.

Other than that, if what I got from the article was what was intended then I liked the idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks to Zolgamax and Sgt_Sargent in chat for the help with strengthening this. Probably the shortest time from conception to posting i've yet dared to attempt. Here's hoping it pays off.
